topics_flat: Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Prime numbers
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Factorization techniques
Number Theory > Diophantine Equations > Techniques: modulo, size analysis, order analysis, inequalities
prompt: A number $n$ is a product of three (not necessarily distinct) prime numbers. Adding 1 to each of them, after multiplication we get a larger product $n + 963$. Determine the original product $n$. (Pavel Novotný)
response_text:
<think>
pqr, (p+1)(q+1)(r+1) - pqr = 963.

Expand: pqr + pq+pr+qr+p+q+r+1 - pqr = pq+qr+rp+p+q+r+1 = 963
So pq+qr+rp+p+q+r = 962.

If all odd, LHS even... pq+qr+rp odd*3=odd, p+q+r odd, sum even. 962 even, ok.
If one is 2: say r=2. Then pq+2p+2q+p+q+2 = pq+3p+3q+2 = 962, pq+3p+3q=960, (p+3)(q+3)=969=3·17·19.
Factor pairs: 3·323, 17·57, 19·51. So p+3,q+3: 17,57 -> p=14 no. 19,51 -> p=16 no. 3·323 -> p=0 no.

If two are 2: p=q=2, r odd. 4+2r+2r+2+2+r=5r+8=962, 5r=954, not integer... wait 954/5 no.

All three 2: 4+4+4+2+2+2=18, no.

If one is 2 doesn't work. Try all odd primes.
pq+qr+rp+p+q+r=962.

Hmm, let me try p=q=r: 3p²+3p=962, no.
Try small: p=3: 3q+3r+qr+3+q+r=qr+4q+4r+3=962, qr+4q+4r=959, (q+4)(r+4)=975=3·5²·13.
Pairs: 15·65 → q=11,r=61. Both prime! Check: 3·11·61=2013. 4·12·62=2976. 2976-2013=963. ✓
Other pairs: 25·39 → 21,35 no. 13·75→9,71 no. 5·195→1,191 no. 75·13 same.

So n=2013.
</think>

Expanding $(p+1)(q+1)(r+1) - pqr = 963$ gives:
$$pq + qr + rp + p + q + r + 1 = 963$$

Testing $p=3$: $(q+4)(r+4) = 975 = 15 \cdot 65$, giving $q=11, r=61$ (both prime).

Check: $3 \cdot 11 \cdot 61 = 2013$ and $4 \cdot 12 \cdot 62 = 2976$, difference $= 963$ ✓

Final answer: 2013
